ontology.foodex2_description: Tomatoes and tomato products — used as closest parent for fresh tomato salad dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 composite dish code exists specifically for tomato salad; A00LC (tomatoes) used as closest parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT does not have a specific tomato salad concept; 226529007 (salad vegetable) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of tomato salad is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 2–3 medium tomatoes sliced and dressed with olive oil and herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170457); Olive oil — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171413); composite estimate for dressed tomato salad
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Italian tomato salad as a composite dish. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy raw tomato (FDC ID 170457) as the dominant ingredient (~85% by weight) combined with a small olive oil dressing contribution (~15% by weight, FDC ID 171413). Energy, macros, fibre, sodium, and key micronutrients are weighted composites. This is a standard minimally processed salad; the estimate is conservative and defensible.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; raw tomato GI = 38 (glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 38 is the published value for raw tomato from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Tomato salad with olive oil dressing is expected to have an equal or lower GI than raw tomato alone due to the fat content of olive oil further slowing gastric emptying; GI 38 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (38 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5 serving factor) / 100 = 2.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content with beneficial potassium
hypertension.guidance: Tomato salad contains approximately 95 mg sodium per 100 g and 237 mg potassium per 100 g, providing a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that supports blood pressure management; individuals with hypertension can include this dish freely as part of a DASH-aligned dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 2.6 per 150 g serving, tomato salad has a minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the olive oil dressing further attenuates glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lycopene, monounsaturated fat from olive oil, and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tomato salad provides lycopene (a cardioprotective carotenoid), vitamin C, and monounsaturated fatty acids from olive oil, with negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-healthy choice consistent with Mediterranean dietary guidelines for cardiovascular disease prevention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato salad (Italian: insalata di pomodori) is a classic Italian antipasto or side dish consisting of ripe, sliced fresh tomatoes dressed with extra-virgin olive oil, fresh basil leaves, salt, and black pepper. Optional additions include garlic, balsamic vinegar, red onion, or mozzarella. The dish is served at room temperature and is prized for its bright, acidic flavour, juicy texture, and simplicity. It is a staple of Italian cucina povera and Mediterranean cuisine. Nutritionally, tomato salad is low in calories (~52 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (~4.5 g per 100 g), and provides a meaningful contribution of vitamin C (13.7 mg), vitamin A (42 mcg RAE), potassium (237 mg), and the antioxidant lycopene from tomatoes. The olive oil dressing contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fats. The dish has a low glycemic index (GI 38) and a very low glycemic load per serving (~2.6), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. In Singapore, this dish appears on Italian café menus and is typically served as a starter or side. The Burmese name in the source record (ခရမ်းချဉ်သီးသုပ်) reflects the tomato salad concept in Burmese, indicating cross-cultural menu labelling in the Singapore food-service context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tomato salad?
Is tomato salad good for diabetics?
Can I eat tomato salad if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in tomato salad?
How is Italian tomato salad made?

tomatoes: ခရမ်းချဉ်သီး

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Italian tomato salad as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy raw tomato (FDC ID 170457) and olive oil (FDC ID 171413) as a weighted composite, which is a defensible and standard approach for a minimally processed salad. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for raw tomato (GI 38), which is a conservative upper-bound estimate for the dressed dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant recipe (dressing ratio, additional ingredients) is unknown.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy: raw tomato (FDC ID 170457, ~85% weight) and olive oil (FDC ID 171413, ~15% weight). No single approved-source entry for Italian tomato salad as a composite dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. USDA SR Legacy is the appropriate secondary global source for this Western dish. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for raw tomato and olive oil per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero in this plant-based, minimally processed dish. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the base recipe. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish; no animal-derived B12 sources present. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for raw tomato and olive oil; no vitamin D-fortified ingredients. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.3), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4), potassium_mg (237.0), calcium_mg (10.0), iron_mg (0.3), vitamin_c_mg (13.7), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (42.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.04), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.02), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.6), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), folate_mcg_dfe (15.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (24.0), magnesium_mg (11.0), zinc_mg (0.2), selenium_mcg (0.4), water_g (90.5) are all reported from USDA SR Legacy composite estimate. 7. The input food_name included a Burmese script annotation (ခရမ်းချဉ်သီးသုပ်) which is the Burmese term for tomato salad; this has been noted in the LLM description for cultural context but is not a mandatory multilingual field under Veritas v2 rules for Italian cuisine. 8. The cuisine is classified as Italian per the input record and restaurant context (The Light Cafe — Italian café menu). Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided as this dish is served in Singapore, supporting multilingual retrieval in the Singapore food-service context. 9. FoodEx2 code A00LC (tomatoes) is used as closest_parent as no composite dish code for tomato salad exists in the FoodEx2 matrix. 10. SNOMED CT code 226529007 (salad vegetable) is used as closest_parent. 11. GL per serving calculated as: GI (38) × carbohydrate_g per 100g (4.5) × serving size factor (150g/100) / 100 = 2.565 ≈ 2.6.
